transcript_segments.text: Every morning before the market opens, I used to spend about an hour doing the same thing.
Opening a stack of research emails and newsletters, trying to piece together what actually mattered for the trading day.
The information was good, but it was scattered, and by the time I'd fully synthesized it, the market was about to open.
So I built a system that does it for me.
Now, every weekday morning, before 8.30am, an AI workflow reads my market research emails, pulls out everything that matters.
Macro context, economic data, stocks in play, key themes, and generates a structured trading briefing, along with a full dash forward that I could scan in just a few minutes.
In this video, I'm going to show you exactly how it works and how you could build your own version, even if you have zero coding background.
I built this myself, and I'm certainly not a developer.
Let me show you what this actually looks like.
This is the daily market rundown the system generates every morning before the open.
At the top, there's a quick market snapshot.
This gives me the key macro setup for the day in just a few sentences.
For example, it highlights the current Fed stance, the most important economic data,
being released that morning in any major geopolitical developments that could influence markets.
Below that is the macro tone section.
This summarizes the most important developments driving the market so I can quickly understand the bigger picture heading into the trading day.
It also expands on the key overnight developments.
That includes things like geopolitical events, commodity moves, or changes in investor sentiment.
Next is the economic calendar.
This highlights the key economic data releases scheduled for the day.

and a label indicating the importance of the release.

and exactly when they're scheduled to hit the market.

will leverage something you have already been doing greatly in your life.

Now this, I believe, is a game changer. The idea of positive trading psychology is that we achieve success not by just getting rid of our negatives, but by building our positives.
When people want to reach out to me as a trading psychologist, a trading coach, what do they want to talk about?
They want to talk about going on till so they want to talk about fear and greed.
Okay, yes, it is important to be able to master those.
But we master those by being grounded in our training and being grounded in our strengths, our talents, which are our inborn strengths, and our skills are acquired strengths.
One of the questions I sometimes ask, because I teach at a medical school, is consider the surgical students and residents.
They're learning surgery. They might be learning orthopedic surgery. They might be learning some other kind of surgery.
But they are in the process of mastering the skills of surgical procedures.
Now I would argue that the risk reward of doing surgery is greater than the risk reward of putting on a trade.
Okay. Why in the, let me guess, in the near 40 years that I have taught at Upstate Medical University in Syracuse, New York, and worked directly with residents and medical students,
why has a surgical student and resident never gone on tilt?
Do you imagine your surgeon, like, yeah, frustrated and going on tilt, I got to make another cut to the fear of missing out.

This is very important, and it's very important to your collaboration with SMB.
Because they are so grounded in training, and the training is occurring, not over a period of days and weeks, but months and years.
They are observing people doing things the right way. They're getting feedback about doing things the right way.
And they're doing it again and again and again and again with supervision until it becomes second nature.
Many, many, many of the problems we think about with respect to trading psychology is simply because we have not put in that training time.
When you do it again and again and again, you see other people doing it again and again, it becomes part of you. It becomes second nature. And you don't go on tilt.

Do you want them to have lots of negative emotion? You want them to be focused.
And that's what positive psychology is all about. Being grounded in your strengths and not just combating the negatives.

One of the big problems that we encounter when we try to make changes is that, you know, we write in a journal, you know, we review some charts.
But there's no emotion behind what we're doing. In the research review I did with my colleagues at SUNY UPS state, we looked at what works in terms of change processes, in terms of therapy.
And it turns out that emotional arousal is the biggest catalyst to change. When we feel something, we're more likely to internalize it.
I've written about the young man I worked with who had patterns of negative thinking. Just was always blaming himself, telling himself how worthless he was. He would never be a success.

month 16 of your trading journey.
You're not completely new anymore, but you're not consistently profitable yet either,
and I'm going to guess you're wondering if you should quit. Maybe there's even a voice in the
back of your head saying maybe you should. Don't. Because what you don't realize is that you're
probably closer than you've ever been. There are five specific signs that appear right before
a trader breaks through to consistent profitability. And if you're experiencing three or more of them
right now, you're only 60 to 90 days away from your first consistently profitable quarter.
Now, I know this because I've experienced all five of these signs in a 60 day window. And 47 days
after that, I had my first really profitable month. Now, let me show you what to look for. Then before

the pattern remains consistent month one through six is like chaos, right? It's a steep learning curve.

Others don't. They start to lose faith, not in their long-term ability, but in the progress they've
already made. They're following rules more consistently, but they're still break even or even
slightly negative. Then comes month 13 through 18. This is the valley. This is where most traders
quit, not because they're failing catastrophically, but because they can't see progress anymore.
They've invested 12 to 18 months, hundreds of hours taken losses. They look at their P&L and think,
I'm no closer than I was six months ago, but that's not really true. They're building something
underneath the surface that doesn't show up in the P&L just yet. Right before they break through,
five specific signs appear. If you're experiencing three or more of these signs right now, do not quit.
You're actually closer than you think. Let me tell you about month 14 for me. I've been trading
for 14 months. I wasn't blowing up, but I wasn't really doing that well either. I was tired.
I was tired of the screen time. I was tired of the journaling. I was tired of the meetings.
I was tired of watching other traders start to make money while I was stuck. Then I had a meeting
scheduled with Bella, with Mike Bella Fjord. Right before that meeting started, I remember thinking,
I was sitting in his office, it was dark, and I was waiting for him to come in. I was like, I think
I'm going to tell him I'm done. I gave this a year and a half. It's not working. He walked in the
light, stayed off. Right? He pulled up my last 60 days of trading, and then I saw this smile come
across his face. I'm sitting there in despair. I'm like, really frustrated, like, about to quit. Right?
And he goes with his smile across his face. You don't see it yet. But you're about to turn the corner.
I'm like sitting there thinking, like, what the hell is he talking about? I'm breaking even at best.
And then he actually broke this same concept down and showed me. He pointed to five things happening
in my trading that I hadn't even noticed. He says, listen, you're starting to get bored at the
right times. You're taking fewer trades. You can articulate your edge clearly. Your losses don't
really wreck your day anymore. You're trading and tracking the right metrics for your trading.
These are the signals. Trust me. Within 60 days, you'll be more profitable than you could imagine.
Right? I walked out of his office, felt a little bit better, felt like that conviction to get back
to work. 47 days later, I had my first really profitable month. And that was proof something
it shifted. All right, sign number one, you're bored in the right way at the right time.
Now, when you first started trading, everything was exciting, right? Every single trade felt like an
event. But as you progress, now certain trades feel a little more mechanical, right? It's the same
setup, similar execution rules or same execution rules, same result. And this trick starts to occur.
You kind of think something's wrong. It's not excitement equals emotion, emotion equals
inconsistency, especially at the wrong times. When you start to have that emotion, when there's
nothing to do, that's you putting yourself out of the market. When you start to not have that
emotion, when there's nothing to do, guess what? Boring equals process, process equals consistency.
The best traders describe trading as boring most of the time. And that is a signal. Sign number two,
you start taking fewer trades, but you're making more money. Now, early on, you're trading
constantly. Later, you start waiting a little bit, right? You stop forcing trades. You focus a
little more on your edge, quality over quantity, because less is more. That is a fascinating thing
that occurs all the time on the desk. You'll see traders starting out. And before they really hit
any sort of metrics that suggest they might be profitable, you'll see 27 symbols traded on a day.
We used to have this joke that you could tell if a guy was going to be profitable or not
profitable on the day, just by the number of shares he traded, just by the number of trades he took.
You didn't even need to see the number of symbols or what was happening. If they took too many
too many trades, too many shares, it was a high likelihood they weren't making money. And then
they were on tilt for like two days after also. When traders start to hone it in, you actually start
to see that selectivity pick up. The per-trade PNL tends to get a little bit skewed. So they're
taking less trades, but they're making more money in the trades that they're taking than they were
before. That's a huge indicator that a lot of people miss, because all you're thinking about is
how much you should have made in the trade versus looking at where you were compared to where you
were before. Pay attention to that sign. Sign number three, and this is the most important one.
You need to be able to describe your edge in one single sentence. If somebody asks you what's your
trading edge? You can answer it instantly, clearly, and specifically. And if you can't articulate
your edge, you typically don't really have one. It doesn't mean you're not on your way to profitability,
but clarity creates confidence. And confidence creates consistency. A lot of times I'll hear a vague
edge like trade momentum stocks, right? That's fine. And I understand that you might trade momentum
stocks, but that's more of a statement than an edge, right? A real edge is specific, measurable,
and repeatable. One sentence, that's the test. So we had this trader who walked in and was like
trading pretty well, starting to show some signs, but ask that same question. All right,
describe to me what your trading edge is. And I didn't get that I trade momentum stocks. I got
like a 45-minute lecture in what this trader's edge was using every indicator. And then I back
tested this and I understand this and I know this and then this and that, that's not an edge.
An edge is often broken down into exactly the dynamics that are happening for the participants that
are playing the game that you're playing right now. And then as a result of that, what should happen
next? It's essentially like saying, I understand what's happening now, but because of that, I understand
what should happen next or what could happen next. If this or this happened. It's not a broad statement
like I'm a trend follower or I'm a momentum trader. Those are just statements, right? This is actually
articulating the situation that gets presented. In Scalparator, we actually built everything as an
individual situation that's going to get presented and then the individual components that are
going to lead into that opportunity. And then most importantly, how that opportunity should play out.
That's an edge, not some vague general statement or some giant lecture about every little nuance
that might occur. Make sure you pass this test. All right, sign number four. The losses don't
wreck your day anymore. All right. So today, I took a big loss right in the open, right? Pretty early
on in the day. It hurt. It sucked, right? But your response is sort of simple. Okay. Next trade,
right? You don't get in there revenge trading. You don't use this emotional spiral anymore. You just
take the rip. Losses become the cost of doing business. But even more importantly, losses become
really critical information to help you adjust what you're doing. Dr. steamburger talks about this
all the time that losses can become losses and they can become traumatic and they can be frustrating
or they can become information. And when they stop bothering you, you actually open the door to
them becoming information. Now, let's all assume that you're taking good losses, right? You're
respecting your edge. Things don't work out in trading. A trade will fail. Some new information will
come out and it'll change what's happening. But the minute that you can really capture the
information that's occurring because of that loss, you're on to something. If you can describe
right after you take a loss, not the feeling and the frustration that you have. But the next three
things that would offer you a trading opportunity based on the information that was presented because
you had a loss, then you really know you're on to something. It doesn't mean you act right away.
It just means you can articulate how that loss becomes information. If you could take multiple losses
and still follow your process, you know you're ready. Sign number five, you're tracking the right
metrics. This is so hard to do early on because we all are so P&L anchored, right? But once you stop
obsessing over your P&L and start tracking your execution, your rule following, your entry
quality, your consistency, the P&L is actually just the output. The execution is the input. You
control the input and the output follows. Now, here's a little metric that you can use that is
totally execution focused. Instead of worrying about the P&L or how much you made in a trade or
didn't make in a trade, start to do this. It's a little game some people play on the desk. I kind of
find it a little weird, but it works. And I've seen it work over and over. So here you go. Traders will
take their entry, what their actual entry was, and then what their ideal exit was. Now, this
implies you actually have a real exit strategy, right? And they will take what their average position
size was or if they only had one in and one out what their core position size was. They'll calculate
how much P&L they should have made had they traded their system perfectly, right? Let's say you bought
it at $5 and you sold it at $6 and you sold it because it broke a 9, it closed below a 9,
and that was your that was your reason to sell. You captured $2 on that move. If that was your
rule and you captured $2, you caught 100% of the available profits to you in that trade. You followed
your trading system, right? If you were late to the trade, you have to start from that initial spot.
If you sold too early, you have to account for how much you actually left on the table or how much
P&L you actually made relative to what you should have done. You know you're onto something if
you're ratio is at like 0.7 or above. Think about that. We're not asking you to be perfect. We're
actually only asking you to get a C grade, but it's really hard to even get that C grade consistently
and if you track it across all of your trades. So think about that. If you can capture 70% of the
actual expected move based on your ideal entry or your actual entry and your real exit,
you're doing pretty well. That's astounding to think about. I don't know anywhere else where you
could get 70% of that and you could have a really successful trading career. It's crazy. Control the
inputs and the outputs follow. Now let's put this all together. Your board, you take fewer trades,
you have a clear edge. The losses don't affect you the same way and then you focus on execution,
not just money. If you're seeing three or more of these signs, you're only 60 to 90 days from

right before this point. I almost did, but don't. Now if you're experiencing these signs,

I want you to go through the five sides and count how many apply to you right now. And if you

now. And if you need a couple of strategies to look at, go check out this other video we did.

transcript_segments.text: Look, we recognize that a lot of you guys watching our channel don't have much in the way of capital
and you can't understand how with such little, in capital and savings,
you could imagine getting involved in trading and growing your net worth substantially.
But that's okay because in this video, we're actually going to be teaching you how to get started
trading even if you only have a tiny amount of capital to get started. And when I say tiny,
I'm talking really tiny, like trades that require under $100 in capital. And we're making this
video because we want to find a way for regular folks who have no experience in trading and
you know limited capital to get started trading. Get started with the experience of trading
so that eventually you could get to the point where you've started making some real money because
you got started small, but you figured out a way to gain a lot of experience trading a very small
account which we found is key to get you trading as soon as you can so you can start to build
that experience base to eventually become potentially a person with major trading skills,
making a great deal of money one day. For me, I made my first trade when I was actually in college
and my net worth was under $2,000. I'm not kidding you. But even if you have less than that,
you can still grow your wealth and you can do that by trading options. You can steadily build your
portfolio until you reach a level where more strategies become available and things can then get
to really open up for you exponentially. In short, today I'm going to show you how to grow a small
account using option strategies that require less than $100 per trade in capital. And we hope that
this can be life changing for you. You see most trading influencers out there will tell you to
buy options. But what they're not telling you is that buying options with a small account is
basically like buying lottery tickets. You might trip or quadruple or quintuple your money or
you might lose everything and quit trading altogether, which is actually the more likely outcome and
the distinct possibility if you understand how quickly and easily options lose their value much
faster than most traders understand. And so we're not going to suggest you go down that road.
To help you to grow your account steadily, the strategy we're going to be teaching today is
not a buying strategy. It's essentially an option selling strategy. This approach is very inexpensive,
capital efficient and designed to help you grow consistently without needing a lot of money.
Now before we jump in, understand this. Growing a small account is challenging. You're limited,
you don't have many strategies available to you. You have to stay focused and you need to manage
positions carefully. But the good news is that we're going to show you exactly how to manage and
close these trades. So let's get into it. I'm Seth Freiberg and I'm the head trader of SMB
Capital's Options Trading Desk here in Manhattan. And one of our jobs in the trading communities to
aware of changes and various offerings in the trading world. And so today we're going to discuss
a topic we've never covered before. And that is trading XSP Index Options. You see many professional
and experienced retail traders trade the S&P 500 Index Options. But the SPX Index is trading
near $7,000 now. And it's gotten so large that it's kind of priced small retail traders out of
the market when it comes to trading option strategies using SPX. And so the XSP Index Options
solve that problem because they are priced at pretty much exactly 10% of the value of the SPX
Index because the XSP Index value is priced at 10% of the SPX Index. And so if the SPX Index
is trading at $6,500, then the XSP Index will be trading at $6,50, and so forth. And so XSP
Index Options have become pretty important for developing options traders. And so we're going to
be looking at a particular option strategy using XSP Index Options. And you'll see just how
little capital you'll need to start trading option strategies to build your wealth. Before we get
into the options trading technique that we're going to be teaching you in today's video, if you're
absolutely brand new to options trading and you don't know much about how options work at all,
we've put together a video for you to understand options basics. And if you click the video appearing
on your screen right now, it will lay the groundwork for you to understand the options strategy
that we'll be sharing with you in this video. Then when you're finished, you can come back and watch
the rest of this video. So let's get started by taking a look at the XSP Index just about a year
ago in April 24th, 2025. A few weeks after the Liberation Day Tire Program was announced by the
White House. And we've superimposed the 20-day moving average of the XSP Index onto the price chart.
And so as you can see, the first day since the beginning of the month that the XSP Index closed
above the 20-day moving average up at 548-48 is this day, April 24th. And so let's say that we're
going to use the 20-day moving average as our criterion for entering and exiting the trade
with very simple rules. If the index closes cleanly above the 20-day moving average,
we're going to start the trade. And if at any point during the trade it closes
below the 20-day moving average line, we just close the trade. Simple enough, right? But how can
we do this for under $100? If the index is priced at over $500? And the answer is that we're going
to implement an option strategy that I can guarantee every time we'll never require more than $100
in capital. And let me show you how it works. So let's pull up an option's chain of the XSP
Index on that day back in April of 2025. And as you can see, that option's chain expires in two
weeks on May 8th. And so we're going to use the first put option strike right below where the
index closed that day at 548-48. So the 548 put is what we're going to be using. And we're going
to go ahead and sell that put for $9.56. And we're going to simultaneously buy the put right below
it at the 547 strike. And we're going to pay $9.18 for that put. And so when you do that,
selling an option higher up on an options chain and buying another option below that on the same
options chain, when you do that, you're entering into what is known as a put credit spread. And since
the put we sold was right below where the market's trading, that's called an at the money put credit
spread. Okay, so now let's take a look at why I say that this could be done for less than $100.
Guaranteed every time. You see, when we sold that 548 put, we received the price of $9.56. But
remember, index options pay off at a rate of $100 per point. So you multiply that by 100. And so we
actually receive $956 in cash in our account. But remember, we also bought the 547 option for 918.
So that costs us 918. And so when you net it down, we received net cash directly into our account
in the amount of $38. And now this is key. Your broker is going to require you to have $62
in capital in your account when you first put this trade on. And the reason for that is that the
largest loss that you could possibly pay out under this trade is $100. If you think about it,
because the long put is only one point below where the short put is. However, you received $38
initially. So the net amount of capital you'll need is only $62. And that's the exact amount
of capital that your broker will require you to have in your account to make this trade.
So that's the capital requirement to put this trade on. Nothing more. So let's now move to the
day that this trade expired May 8th. And as you can see, the price of the index stayed above the
20-day moving average during the entire course of the trade, closing on expiration date at a price
of $566.39. And so what does that mean for the outcome of our trade? Well, starting with the $38
we initially received when we first sold the spread. And when you consider the fact that both
of the put options expired way below the closing price of the index on expiration date down at
5.48 and 5.47, then obviously those both expire worthless. And therefore the trade profit is
the initial cash you received when you first sold the spread. In other words, $38. And so let's say
that we decide to make this trade every time we see the XSP index close below the 20-day moving
average and then break above it and close above it. And we trade this every time it happens consistently
over time. Well, the next time that happened was on August 4th of that year, about three months later,
whereas you can see it had slipped below the 20-day moving average on the prior trading day and then
subsequently broke above it on the next trading day closing at $6.32.99. So that's our signal to
initiate the trade. So again, we pull up an option chain expiring two weeks later on August 18th
and we sell the put right below where the index closed just like before. We sell the 6.32 put
for 557 and we buy the 6.31 put right below it for 5.24. So now we've created an at-the-money put
credit spread just as we did in the last example. And as you can see, in this case, we collected $33.
And in this case, your broker will require $67 of capital to execute this trade. So again,
these trades are going to require well under $100 to execute. And these amounts will vary depending
on market conditions, but typically the credit you'll receive will be around the $30 to $40
range and the amount of capital required will be in the $60 to $70 range. And so let's take a look
at the price chart of the XSP index on the day this trade expires, which is August 18th. And as you
can see, the index continued to rally after trade entry closing at 6.44.91 on the day this put
credit spread expired. And so just like the initial trade that we covered, the index closed well
above both the 6.31 and 6.32 puts resulting in a $33 profit on this trade. Now, the next time this
trade signal took place was actually in that next week where the index briefly closed below the
20 day moving average and powered up far above the 20 day moving average, the next day closing at
6.46.69. And by now you probably get the drill. So we're going to pull up the September 5th option
chain expiring 14 days. And we're going to sell the 6.46, 6.45 for put credit spread again right at
the money collecting this time $37. As you can see from the calculation, with a $63 capital
requirement this time, but this time something unexpected happened. And that is that on September
2nd, 2025, the index gap down and close below the 20 day moving average. And as a result, our rules say
that we get out of the trade. We exit the trade at that point with the index closing at 6.41.55.
Now, so let's see what that means to us. At the close on September 2nd, here's what was going on
with our trade the 6.46 put, which we originally sold for 468. Now that is spiked to 602. And the 6.45
put is now trading at 5.42. And so the calculation at the end of the trade goes like this. You see,
to close the put credit spread, you need to buy back the put you sold and sell off the put you bought.
So as you can see from the calculation, after taking into consideration the initial $37 of cash flow,
we had to buy back the 6.46 put for 602, which therefore cost the $602. But on the other hand,
we were able to sell off the 6.45 put for 5.42. And so netting it all down, there was a loss on this
trade of $23. And that's going to be a very common occurrence. When you lose the trade, the loss
will frequently be lower than the average win, which is a very good feature for systematic options
like the one we're sharing with you today. Okay. And so it's not to be too tedious. We've created
this table, which provides the results of all of the occasions where our signal fired off. And we
executed and at the money put credit spread from that first April trade that we showed you at the
beginning of the video through to today. And while most of the trades were wins, there were three
losses as you see. But if you scrutinize this table, you'll see that in each case, the losses
were lower than each of the individual wins. And so when we net it all down, the result is a
profit of $187, which comes out to a little more than a 62% return on the initial starting capital
of $300. And of course, we didn't need that $300 actually for any one trade. But with a trading
system like this, it's a good idea to start out with multiples of the amount of capital necessary
for any individual trade. So that if you do have a series of losses in a row, you'll still be in
a position to continue trading your system without having to add capital. And so to be conservative,
we use $300 as the denominator of the return calculation. And that's how we got that percentage.
And so what I'd like you to take away from today's video is that you can begin to establish your
trading career with very little capital and the potential with well-designed option strategies
to achieve exceptional returns far beyond what you could do with conventional investments like
buying ETFs of major market indexes like Spy, which have much lower returns, usually close to
10% or even less per year. And once you start achieving exceptional returns, the compounding
effect on your account will be remarkable. And I can tell you that proprietary trading firms like
Gars become very interested in traders who can produce consistent levels of profitability
without substantial drawdowns regardless of the amount of capital that you are personally trading.
If you show discipline, maturity, and a firm understanding of options in your strategy,
that is far more important to us than the amount of zeros that you have in the capital base
that you're trading from. You can start your trading journey right now, establishing your track
record for a career in trading if you're careful and deliberate about what you're doing.
I hope this video inspires you to move forward in your trading career and not be discouraged
by your current capital level. Some of the greatest traders here at SMB started out with almost nothing
personally and now some of them have ended up as seven and even eight figure a year traders.
Now if you'd like to learn three more options strategies that our pro traders use including
the unique options trick that allows you to make money while you wait to buy stock sur ETFs
at the price you want and the options income strategy that allows you to make consistent money whether
the market goes up or down or sideways and how to make money on a stock or index trade even if
you're wrong on the direction then click the link that's appearing right now at the top right hand
corner of your screen. That will open up the free workshop registration page in a new window so
don't worry you won't lose this video or you can register directly for free at optionsclass.com.

transcript_segments.text: Trading is supposed to be a lone wolf pursuit. That's the image everyone has in their head.
One trader, one screen, one great trade.
If you've ever read reminiscences of a stock operator, you probably imagine Jesse Lippermor
standing there, leading the tape, seeing something nobody else can see.
That mythology is part of what pulls a lot of us into trading in the first place.
But the reality, at least for me, has been very different.
Some of the best trades I've ever been a part of didn't come from one brilliant idea they came from collaboration.
One trader finds the signal, another trader digs into the catalyst.
Someone else reads the tape and says this is working.
And suddenly an idea that started small turns into a fully formed trade that none of us would have executed the same way alone.
Last week at our firms trading summit, I gave a talk about this exact idea.
The power of collaboration and why the best trading teams often outperform the lone wolf model.
The response from the room is incredible.
And it made me realize how many traders are still trying to do everything themselves.
So today we're going to unpack that topic a little more.
What makes a great trading team?
How do different cognitive strengths actually complement each other?
And if you're trading alone right now, how do you start building the kind of collaboration that can actually improve your results?
Welcome back to the Trading Floor podcast.
I'm Garrett Dryden. I'm here with Tim and Kurt as always behind the scenes.
So Tim, what's going on, man?
I want to ask you a question right away because you were not at the summit.
You were skiing in Colorado, which I'm not going to hate you for because that sounds like a pretty good deal.
But you actually have like you didn't you didn't hear my speech.
You weren't there.
But you know a lot about this subject.

In fact, I think you might know more than anybody on the subject because I've seen you through the course of your career here at S&B collaborate with probably more people than I've ever seen anyone collaborate with.
And some of these have worked out great and some of them have not.
And so I want to ask you, what do you think makes a successful trading collaboration?
Yeah, you're definitely right.
I have experience with team trading and just to mention too, you know, we're on a team together and we're trading every single day.
First off, congrats on the summit.
Both you and Kurt heard it was a smashy success.
And I actually brought some questions for you to dive into it.
Great.
Yeah, what makes what makes a good collaboration like one right off the bat, you do want to be trading similar playbooks.
And you don't necessarily have to have the exact same playbook or exact same style of trading, but you do need some cohesion on what you're trading.
But you want to have differing strengths.
And I think that's the biggest thing where you want it to have each person bringing something new to the table.
And in a certain trade, like you said in the intro, different aspects that they are the best at and they could really take lead on.
So right off the bat, I would say those are the two biggest and along with that, you want people that are hungry, you want decision makers, you want people who want the ball in their hands.
But I could go on and on, but I would say the two biggest right off the bat is similar playbooks.
You're watching the same stuff and you have very defined setups within your team playbook and then differing strengths that together make a great team.
Alright, so you mentioned the differing strengths thing, which I think is huge.
You didn't mention, mention communication skills, which I think is a huge one that will, you know, we'll get to.
I'll ask you about that because I think that's big.
But back to the differing strengths now at the summit, I talked about the fact that some of us are really great at.
High bandwidth like we can manage a lot of information at once. We can manage a lot of positions at once.
Some of us are very quick thinkers.
Right, we can we can process information really quickly.
Some of us are analytical. We can go really deep.
Some of us are great idea generators.
Some of us are great trade and risk managers.
Some of us can execute really well.
Some of us understand the narrative better than anybody else.
Right, it is really hard to be elite at all of those things.
Right, like you're I mean, we want to be elite traders.
That's what we're doing here.
But I would argue that you don't have to be elite at every single one of those categories to be an elite trader.
The same way that if you're in the MBA, like if you're an elite rebounder and defender, like you don't have to be an elite score.
You could be okay, right?
That's it's the same thing, right?
And so my argument for why every single trader should consider at least.

Like how we get into like how to do that.
My argument is that is that like you don't have to be the best at all of those categories.

videos.description: SMB Capital analysis of QHvp6sja. We discuss market trends, technical indicators, and investment strategies. Don't miss this comprehensive breakdown of market opportunities.

transcript_segments.text: I can tell when a trader is just two weeks away from blowing up their account.
Here are the seven red flags I see every single time.
And if you have even three of these, you just need to stop trading immediately.
Not next week.
Don't wait till tomorrow.
Right now.
Because last month I watched three traders completely ignore these signals.
And all three of them are just on the sidelines right now.
This is your warning system.
Pay attention.
Now before we go through the seven red flags, I need you to understand something.
If you recognize yourself in these, you're not a bad trader.
You're just in a dangerous pattern.
And patterns can be broken, but only if you catch them early.
Most traders don't see these red flags until after the damage is done.
That realization kicks in of what just happened.
By the time they see what's happening, their accounts are already gone.
So as we go through these, I want you checking yourself honestly.
One or two red flags, yellow alert.
You can start to make some changes now.
Three or four red alert.
You should step back immediately.
Five or more, you should stop trading.
Full stop.
I'll give you the protocol at the end for these.
But let's start with red flag number one.
Our win rate is going up, but your account isn't.
This sounds backwards right?
You're winning on more trades.
Shouldn't that mean you're making more money, but not necessarily.
Here's actually what winds up happening.
You're cutting your winners short and you're letting your losers run.
So you're winning 70% of your trades, but your average winner is $200.
Your average loser is like $700.
Seven winners times $214 of winning.
Three losers times $700, $2100 of losing.
Your net is negative $700.
In this case, 70% win rate, which sounds amazing, is still losing money.
This is one of the most dangerous patterns because it feels like you're improving.
You're winning more, but your account is telling you the truth here.
This is what this really big red flag reveals.
Your trading for emotional validation, not for profit.
You never want to be trading for emotional validation.
You want to be trading for profits.
And this is the fix if you find yourself in this situation.
You want to track your average win versus your average loss.
If your average loss is bigger than your average win and your win rate is above like 60%
you're in trouble.
Red flag two is simply about volume and this one sneaks up on you.
Red flag two is your trading more than on your best days.
So think about your best trading day ever.
How many trades did you take?
For most of the traders on our desk, I'm guessing two, three, maybe even four.
But now think about last week, how many trades are you taking each day now?
Sometimes it's 10, 15, 20.
This winds up being the over trading trap and it's a death spiral.
Here's what happens.
You take a loss.
You want to make it back.
So you force a trade that's not really there that then loses.
Now you're down more.
So you take another trade and another or another.
By the end of the day, you've taken 18 trades and you're down more than you started.
What this reveals is your revenge trading.
You're addicted to the action and that addiction gets triggered when you start to lose
because you're trading to get the action to win.
As soon as you find yourself revenge trading, you start to lose that emotional control.
That's where that slippery slope gets kicked in.
Sometimes you can feel it in your body.
That energy of like, I can't believe I lost on that.
The fix for this is go back to what that top day looked like.
Set a max number of trades per day.
Three trades, four trades, five trades, whatever your best days look like.
Set that as the number of bullets you have on the day.
If you hit your limit, if you hit that number of trades, that's it.
You're done.
Even if you see another setup, you have to walk away.
You have to stay engaged with the market, but you have to stay.
You have to walk away from your trading station.
The key on this is to focus on who am I at my best and how do I get back to that?
This is a permanent solution.
This is a temporary solution, but it's a temporary solution that is going to force you
to honor the number of trades that make you your best.
You're going to get back in that groove and start to make good trading decisions again.
Red flag three, it's subtle, but it's deadly.
You can't remember your last three trades.
Right now, without looking at your platform,
can you tell me what were your last three trades?
In the chat, pause the tape right after this.
In the chat, you should be able to tell me what the setup was,
what your entry logic was, and what your exit logic was.
And if you can't answer that, if you can't go post it in the chat right now,
you're not trading, you're just reacting.
There's a difference between being in the zone, like in that groove where things are feeling well,
you're taking in the information, and then just being in chaos.
In the zone, you're calm, you're intentional.
Every trade you take has a reason.
In chaos, you're just moving too fast.
You're taking trades because they're there, not because they fit your plan.
Right?
This red flag reveals you've abandoned your process, you're chasing the market.
When you start chasing the market, there's a clean and clear solution to that.
The fix is after every trade before you take another one.
Write it down.
One, what was the setup?
Two, why did I take it?
Three, how do I feel right now?
You have to do this before you take another trade.
If you can't articulate those three things, don't take the next trade.
All right, red flag four is about strategy hopping.
I see this destroy accounts all the time.
I mean, you're adding new strategies as soon as an old one stops working.
Maybe your momentum strategy hasn't worked in like two or three weeks.
So you start learning meme reversion.
Your meme reversion stops working.
So you try breakout trading.
Breakout stop working.
So you see the pattern.
Here's the problem.
The strategies actually don't stop working.
If you learn a diverse set of strategies, they're not going to stop working.
The market conditions are going to change.
Your momentum strategy is going to work in trending markets.
It should actually stop working in choppy markets.
That's not a strategy problem.
That's a market condition problem.
But instead of recognizing that, you tend to abandon the strategy and start learning
a new one.
And by the time you've mastered that new strategy,
the markets changed again.
And now your old strategy would be working.
It's a really tough situation.
But what it reveals is you're actually confusing the market conditions
with your strategy failure.
Overarching theme you lack patience.
You're looking for the perfect system.
We built Scalparator with five different trades because
there are different market environments.
Different trades work in different market environments.
You need a diverse understanding of strategies
so that you can do what's working in this market environment.

An opening range break in ORB.
We can even take a look at Spy.

This trade used to work incredibly well, especially on a gap out of range

We need to go higher because people were chasing things.

they didn't get in on the day period.

Don't do all the work and then just say,
oh, that's never going to work again and go in a completely new direction.
Don't abandon it.
Wait for the market conditions to shift back.
If you don't have another strategy,
then at least trade smaller size.
But really avoid strategy hopping.
Let the market guide you as to which strategies you should be trading.
Red 5 is the most dangerous one.
This is where accounts actually blow up.
And let me tell you what this looks like because I've seen this pattern
truly in careers.
The red flag is you start to justify bigger losses.
And it's a really slippery slope.
A couple years ago I was coaching a trader.
We'll call him Ryan.
Right?
Smart guy, disciplined.
He'd been profitable for I think eight months straight.
His max loss per trade was like around $1,000.

and then quickly it was coming back against him.

transcript_segments.text: There's a powerful option strategy called the wheel that I want to teach you in today's video.
And candidly, the wheel can beat the hell out of the returns of the equity markets if you understand
how and when to use it. I can show you how you can squeeze tons of extra profit out of trading,
solid stocks. And the best part is that the concepts are so simple, you'll find it hard to believe.
So if that's of interest to you, then stick around because I think you're going to be surprised
at how effective this can be and how easy it is to learn. Today, we're going to be talking about
the wheel and I'll be explaining why it's called that in a minute. But first, I want to do a really
brief review of some basic concepts. So if you're brand new to options, you'll understand what we're
covering here. And if not, then it will just be refresher to make sure that you're crystal clear
on the concepts. So first of all, when trading options, you can buy call options or you can buy
put options. But it's also important to realize that you can buy or sell them. Your broker will
allow you to do both. Now, when you buy a call, you have the right to buy 100 shares of a stock at
the call strike price. Whereas when you're selling a call, you have the obligation, not the right
this time, but the obligation to sell 100 shares of a stock at the call strike price. Now, when you
buy a put, you have the right to sell 100 shares of a stock at that put strike price. And when you
sell a put, you have the obligation to buy 100 shares of a stock at the put strike price.
Now, if you think I'm going too fast or this is confusing, then that's fine. We created a video
for you to understand options basics. And if you click the video appearing on your screen right
now, it will lay the groundwork for you to understand the wheel strategy that we're going to be
teaching you in today's video. Then when you're finished, you can come back and watch the rest of
this video with that is background. Let's talk about the wheel option strategy. Now, first of all,
the idea behind the wheel strategy is to get paid while you wait to buy a stock at the price
you want to pay for it. And incidentally, this is actually a strategy that Warren Buffett uses to
get into stocks that he loves at his price. He uses this strategy all the time as a matter of fact.
The most important thing is, first of all, you need to find a stock that you want to own.
That is the basic prerequisite for this whole strategy. You have your eye on a stock, but you
think that it's currently too expensive in today's prices. So, for example, I thought we could take
a look at the ETF QQQ known to traders as the Qs. That's the ETF, which is really a basket of stocks
containing the components of the Nasdaq 100 index. And let's face it long term, it wouldn't be a
bad idea to own at the right price, of course, the basket of stocks that are clearly driving the
future of mankind, which are tech stocks. And so, for example, let's head back to October 17th of last
year, 2025. And as you can see, the Qs had been in a really nice uptrend off the tariff panic lows of
April 2025. And it had traded as high as 613 before sharp pull back just a week before,
where it sold off down to 589, then rally back over 600, closing at 603, 93 that day.
Now, let's just say, for example, here that you want to buy it when it retraces to a price of
595, believing that this uptrend is likely to continue. So, let's say you don't want to chase it,
you want the stock to come back to you, and you never want to buy a stock at the high. So,
it's always a good idea when you're buying stocks to buy it when they're retracing if it all possible.
So, let's say we want to buy 100 shares of the Qs when they come back to 595. The key is that
we have a longer term perspective on the Qs, and it doesn't matter, could be Amazon, or Walmart,
or Caterpillar, Tesla, or J.P. Morgan, or whatever, it doesn't matter. It's the same technique.
Okay, so we picked October 17th because it was the third Friday in October, and so for any stock,
there's always going to be an options expiration on the third Friday of each month. And so,
we're going to be selling a put at that price, 595, about a month out on the third Friday of November,
which was November 21st. And remember, when you sell a put, then you are obligated to buy 100
shares of the stock at that strike price if the shares close lower than that on the day that put
option expires. So, when we pull up an options chain which expires of November 21st, you'll see that
we are selling the 595 strike price because this would mean that we have to buy the shares when
they drop back to 595. So, we sell the 595 put with an expiration of November 21st, which, as you
can see, is going for 1249. And since options sell in units of 100, this means that we are receiving
cash because we are selling it, and in this case, we are receiving $1249. And incidentally, you're
going to need to have $59,500 in your account to do this, and the reason is that you may need to
buy those shares at 595 per share if your put gets triggered. So, keep that in mind.
So, now let's move to the day this trade expires on November 21st. And as you can see, the Q's after
rallying all the way up to $637 pulled back pretty hard, closing the day, the put option expired at
590.07. And so, what does that mean? Well, it means that we now own 100 shares of the Q's at 595,
but it also means that we made $1249 from that put that we sold because even though we got to
sign those 100 shares, we still get to keep that $1249 no matter what. So, we made $1249 in 35 days,
which is actually a great return in such a short period of time if you do the math. It's actually
about an annualized return of 20%. But be that as it may, now that we own the shares, this is where
the wheel strategy kicks in. Because this is when according to the wheel strategy, you'll start selling
calls. Now, what do I mean by that? Well, when we sell a call, it means that we will have the
obligation to sell those 100 shares of the Q's that we were just assigned at the strike price
of the call. And so, what we would do here is we'd sell a call that is approximately 25 points
above the price that we were assigned those shares so that we give the stock some room to run
up before we are obligated to sell it. So, we'll sell the 620 call, which is 25 points above our
acquisition price of $595. And we sell that again on the third Friday of the next month,
which is December. So, that would be on December 19th. And as you can see, that's selling for
price of $4.90. So, this means that we would receive $490 in cash for that call option.
And now, again, the question is, what happens here after this option, this call option expires.
So, the first option expired after 37 days. So, then in another 28 days, in this case, we'll see
what happens. And so, as it turns out on December 19th, the Q's rallied and close that day at 617-05.
And so, what that means is that the 620 call option expired worthless because it has no value
unless the stock closes above its strike price, which is 620. And so, that option just dies and goes
away. But think about it. The option died, but you collected $490 at the beginning. And guess what,
you'd get to just pocket that $490. And so, let's keep track of what has happened so far.
We pocketed $1249 in options cash flow in November and another $490 in December,
resulting in a total of $1,739. So, so far, you know, after just two months, which is still a great
annualized return, about 16% if you do the math. Okay. So, when this happens, we just run it right
back and sell that same 620 call into the third Friday in January, which is January 16th,
which is when we decided earlier we'd be happy to sell the shares. But you'll notice in this case
that the price is a lot higher, $989. And that's because as the stock price approaches to call
strike price, the more likely it is that the stock will just blast through that price over the next
month. And so, the market is going to charge a lot more for the right to buy at that price. And so,
that's actually good news for us because we are selling that call. And so, in this case, as you can
see, we'll be collecting $989 for the January trade. And remember, once you've received that premium,
nobody can take it away from you. That premium is yours and the cash is deposited directly into
your account. It's yours forever. Okay. So, let's move to the day that the January trade
expired. And as you can see, the queues rallyed a bit more here, closing at $6.21.05. So, when this
happens, you are required to sell the shares at the strike price of the call. So, you've got to sell
them at $6.20. And so, when that happens, and you no longer have those shares in your account,
they are automatically sold by your broker at the call strike price of $6.20. Now, I'd like to
stop here, though, and ask you to think about something. We've been focusing on all the options
premium we've been collecting. And that has been impressive on its own. But something else just
happens, something that's actually a bit more significant. And that is that through this process
of collecting those premiums, we also managed to buy the queues at a very low price, $5.95,
because we were patient and incidentally got paid cash for waiting for our price. And that's what
we got from those put premiums. But now, on top of that, we just sold those shares 25 points higher
at $6.20. And so, if you do the math, we made a profit of $2,500 on those shares. And so, let's
update our results table, and as you can see, in January, there are actually two entries,
the options profit, but also the share profit from BIMELOW and SELLING HIGH, which is the whole idea
behind the wheel strategy. You see, the wheel strategy has both components. You make money booking
positive cash flow for options premium, and you make money by getting into stocks at really
great prices. This is why the strategy is so attractive. Okay, so, we sold a put collecting cash
from that. The shares got assigned to us at a great price. We started selling calls, getting cash
from those, and then the shares got assigned away due to our short calls at a great profit. So,
where do we go from here? Well, why do you think it's called the wheel? It's called the wheel,
because we sell below the market. The market rotates up to a higher price, and we sell it,
and then we rotate back down to the put side, and what do we do? We start all over again,
and sell a put below where the stock price is, hoping to catch another opportunity to buy it at
a great price, and do it all over again. And guess what? If we never get the opportunity,
we're still getting paid for trying every month, because when we sell that next put for February,
after our calls are gone, and after the shares are gone, once that happens, and we sell the put,
we get paid again, in this case, another $5.85 right down at the same strike price where we
acquired the shares originally at that $5.95 put strike price, and we're collecting $585 in cash.
And so, on February 20th, just last week, as you can see, the queue is closed at 60881,
so it pulled back a bit from the previous month, and so what that means is that our put down at 595
expires worthless, and so that put just dies, and we just add that cash to our profit,
onto our profit table, which brings our grand total of wheel strategy profits to $5,813.
Now, I'd like you to focus on a very important reality in all this, which you might have missed,
and that is that we implemented the wheel strategy and made $5,813, but did you ever think about
what would have been the outcome, with basically the same capital had we just bought the shares
of the queues back in October when we started the program when the queues were trading at 603-93.
Well, they closed at 60881 on February 20th, so your gain on those shares would have been
$488, but with the wheel strategy, we made $5,813, more than 11 times as much profit over that same
four-month period, because at the end of the day, the wheel strategy is a strategic use of options
to take advantage of price movements, even on stocks, which have hardly moved at all at the
end of the day. And so what I'd like you to take away from today's video is that the wheel strategy
is a very powerful option strategy that allows you to monetize the movements of solid stocks
so that you're always creating cash flow opportunities, and in cases like this example,
making a terrific return on a stock that basically didn't move at all. This is the kind of out-of-the-box
strategy that options can open up for you once you begin to learn how options work. And when you
do, the opportunities for pulling cash out of the markets becomes almost unlimited. Learning how
to trade options is a great skill to have if you're interested in creating cash flow from the equity
markets, even when they're hardly moving at all. Now, if you'd like to learn three more options
strategies that our pro traders use, including the unique options trick that allows you to make money
while you wait to buy stocks or ETFs at the price you want, and the options income strategy that
allows you to make consistent money whether the market goes up or down or sideways, and how to make
money on a stock or index trade, even if you're wrong on the direction, then click the link
that's appearing right now at the top right hand corner of your screen. That will open up the
free workshop registration page in a new window so don't worry you won't lose this video,
or you can register directly for free at optionsclass.com.

transcript_segments.text: Hi, I'm Seth Freiberg, head trader of SMB Capital's Options Trading Desk here in Manhattan.
And today I'm going to be showing you how options traders at prop firms make money as professional traders.
And you know, when I tell people that I'm a professional trader and that the way I generate income and build wealth is by trading options,
people give me this look like this kind of polite hesitancy.
And I know that they're thinking, hey, this guy is just gambling. He's just depending on luck.
And he's just been lucky so far and when his luck changes, he's in for a world of hurt.
And you know, it'll probably shock you to hear me say this, but in a way, that statement's true.
Trading is in a way gambling and the market is in a way a giant legal casino.
Why would I say that? After all, I am a professional trader myself and I make a living trading.
So why would I say something like that? Well, let me ask you this very simple question.
At a casino, who are the participants that always lose money? And the answer is the players, the gamblers who come to the casino,
the customers of that casino. You see the gamblers, the customers, sometimes they'll win, sometimes they'll lose,

transcript_segments.text: I read an article this week about a trading community of about a thousand members who all piled into a specific options trade every day.
And in fact, at least one member had risked his entire life savings to trade this particular strategy.
And the reports are that the strategy was working out fine. It was doing great. Everybody was happy.
But then seemingly out of nowhere, late last year, to their horror, the active members of the community apparently experienced
more than a $50 million loss over like a four or five day period.
And remember, this was a strategy that was reportedly performing really well before this happened.
And the guy who had risked his entire life savings, and there might have been more, I don't know, but that guy actually had to launch a GoFundMe page,
soliciting donations to help him cover his basic living expenses. And this is really sad actually.
But you've got to ask yourself, how the hell does this happen? I mean, the strategy is doing really great one day.
And the next day, all these people are going broke from that same strategy.
So what is the lesson we can learn from this? How does this happen?
Well, we're going to do a deep dive into how something like this can happen.
And I'm doing this because I want everyone watching this video to promise me you'll never get involved in any approach that sounds anything like this.
If you do, well, stick around and wait and see what could happen to you.
I'm Seth Furberg, I'm the head trader of SMB Capital Soptions Trading, Desk here in Manhattan.
And every once in a while, a catastrophe, a totally avoidable disaster takes place in the trading world.
And it's important for our community that follows our YouTube channel to be warned about this kind of thing so that you don't make this terrible mistake yourself.
And that's what today's video is going to be about.
Now, we're going to explain how this happened in this video and it involves a particular options income strategy.
And so if you're absolutely brand new to options trading and you don't know much about options and how they work, we've created a video for you to understand options basics.
And if you click the video appearing on your screen right now, it will lay the groundwork for understanding the options strategy that we'll be discussing in today's video.
Then when you're finished, you can come back and watch the rest of this video.
So first off, I need to say that I'm not privy to the precise trace that these people were doing each day.
I have a general idea of what they were doing enough to understand how this whole thing blew up and will run you through that in a minute.
So today's explanation is not going to be a factual breakdown of what happened in this case.
Instead, the example we're going to run you through today is a kind of a rough simulation estimate of what we understand happened to these poor folks.
And so the purpose of this video is not to issue an actual report on the loss these folks experience.
Rather, we want to run you through the math of why the basic principle upon which their trading strategy was premised had an enormous risk embedded in it.
So that if you ever come across ideas like this in the future, you'll understand just how catastrophic these ideas can become.
And so stay very, very far away from them.
Okay, and so the way that this trade works sometime near the closing bell, the traders pull up an options chain of the SPX index, the S&P 500 index, an options chain expiring the next day.
So in other words, what's called a one DTE options chain.

And then the trader proceeds to construct what options traders refer to as an iron condor, which in this case, we call a market neutral iron condor because the way it's positioned, it expresses no bias.

It's to what the direction of the index will be headed that next day is. In other words, the traders in different as to whether the index goes up or down as long as it doesn't go too far in either direction.
And so let's run through an example, which I believe to be around the same time frame as this catastrophe, you know, that happened to these folks.
Okay, so as I said, this strategy utilizes SPX index options.
So let's take a look at the daily chart of the SPX index back on December 18, 2025, right before Christmas.
As you can see, the index has been rallying all year off of its April lows, closing at 67, 74, 76 that day.
And so when you execute this particular option strategy, the iron condor, you actually collect cash upfront.
And the goal is basically to hold on to as much of that cash as possible. And how this happens will become clear to you as we go through the example.

So just hang in there. From what I understand, once the traders pull up that options chain expiring the next day, they would look at the call side and the put side on that options chain and try to find a combination of options where they would collect a net credit of $1.75 for each lock that they sold.
So for example, let's take a look at this options chain from 4 p.m. on December 18.
As you can see with SPX closing right around 67, 75, if you move up the options chain on the call side, you'll see that we've outlined both the 68, 10 and the 68, 15 calls.
And on the put side, we've outlined the 67, 45 and the 67, 40 puts.
If we just focus on the prices of those options, you'll see that if we had sold the 68, 10 call, bought the 68, 15 call, sold the 67, 45 put and bought the 67, 40 put.
If you net it all down, it comes to $1.80. That means the criterion of receiving at least $1.75 when you enter into this iron condor.
So let's suppose that the members of this trading community collectively sold 9,000 of these iron condors that day.
There were 1,000 of them, as I said, so it's very possible that the collective number could have been about 9,000 of these iron condors.
So the collective options chain for the community would look like this that day.
Selling 9,000 of the 68, 10 calls, buying 9,000 of the 68, 15 calls, selling 9,000 of the 67, 45 puts and buying 9,000 of the 67, 40 puts.
What happens cash flow wise when you do this? Well, as you can see, we sold the 68, 10 calls for price of 3, 45 but index pay options pay off at a rate of $100 per point.
And so you multiply that by 100 and we sold 9,000 of them. So when you multiply it all together, they would have received from selling those 9,000 calls, $3,100, $50,000.
But then you turn right around and pay out $2,385 for buying the 9,000 calls up at 68, 15 for protection, as you can see from that same calculation.
Then simultaneously we sold the 67, 45 puts bringing in 6,000,000,000 and as you can see from the calculation, we also pay out 5,000,000 for those protective 67, 40 puts.

So when you net it all down, it results in positive cash flow of a million 6, 20 for which their brokers collectively would require 2,880 in capital to execute this trade using this 9,000 lot trade size that we did here because that represents the worst case scenario loss on this trade.

Let's first talk about the desired outcome of this trade. You see, you've received a million 6, 20 in cash and the goal of the trade is to hold on to as much of that cash as possible by the end of the next day when the options expire.
The best outcome, the outcome where you get to keep all the cash, is if the index were to close at any price between the calls we sold at 68,000 and above the puts we sold at 67, 45, why?

Because at any price between those two points, all four options expire worthless. That's because calls only have value if the closing price on expiration day is above the call strike price or in the case of puts below that put strike price.

But if the index ends up closing between those two points anywhere in that 65 point range between 67, 45 and 68, 10, then all four options would expire worthless because neither the calls nor the puts would have value in that range.

But there's another outcome and an example of that is what happened on the next day when this trade expires and all the options can be assigned their final value.

And so as you can see the next day, December 19, SPX staged a big rally closing up about 60 points to 68, 35, 50. Now at this point as we said, we're in a position where we can value each of the options because they've expired.

So let's take a look at that and you'll probably be pretty shocked to see that while you start with a million 6, 20 of initial cash you received, take a look at those 68, 10 calls that you sold to value a call at expiration.

Now you did also buy the 68, 15 calls and you're able to recover from your loss a lot, 17,550, which is what those calls are worth that expiration for the same reason.
To value the 68, 15 calls you subtract out the price of SPX from 68, 15 and you get 1950 for those.

So when you multiply that out it becomes a total value of 17,550 that your broker is going to cash in for you automatically and of course both the 67, 45 and 67, 40 puts expire worthless because they are way below the SPX closing price of 68, 30, 450.
And so when you net it all down it results in a loss to the community of $2,880,000.
Okay, so that's not a very good start, but the really bad part hasn't even been covered yet.

So let's get into that. You see the strategy that this group is apparently employing is a strategy based upon a money management technique that's actually used by gamblers called the Martin-Gale system.

And the idea is that anytime you experience a loss you simply put on a larger trade the next day where the initial cash received for selling the iron condor exceeds the loss from the previous days so that if the index closes between the calls and the puts that next day you get to keep that cash and you be up money after two days even though you had a big loss the previous day.

So in this case they need to collect more than 2,880, obviously when they entered into that next day's iron condor. Well to accomplish this the next day the trade would have required an increase in the lot size to 16,000 lots as you can see.
Why do I say that? Well if we look at the prices we got for each of these options you can see that we're talking some pretty big numbers here because the condor price per lot in this case was $1,550.

So that exceeds our minimum $1,75. And if you do the math a 16,000 lot iron condor comes to $2,960,000 in cash collected from selling that condor which exceeds the loss we had on the first trade by a bit which is how we got to that amount of lots.

And by the way in this case the communities broker collectively required 5,040 in capital for this second new trade but when we take a look at what happened the next day the index rallied again the next day up 44 points this time to 68,78,49.

Well how did our trade do? Well not too great because again starting with the initial 2,660,880 would have collected the index blue far past the calls they sold at 68,65, forcing them to pay out over $21 million for those although they recover over $13 million from the calls they bought up at 68,70 but that wasn't nearly enough obviously and the traders would have lost $5,040,000 that day.

So now keeping track of where we are you can see that we're now up to total losses of 7,920,000 dollars. And so we need to overcome that with the cash flow from the next days trade and so in this case we have to put on a 42,000 lot iron condor.

Why? Well if we do the math we realize that with each slot bringing in a dollar 90 that we be needing 42,000 lots to get up to 7,980 and positive cash flow which again slightly exceeds our loss from the first true trades.

Unfortunately the next day the index rallied again closing is the 909.79 that day which is above the location of both of the calls and so when we do the calculation as you can see we've now added another $13 million,20,000 to our growing pile of losses which now totals over $20 million.
And so once again as the system dictates the traders entered the next days trade with now because the massive losses would have required a 105,000 lot iron condor.
Now more than 11 times larger than the first trade just three days ago by the way which produces 21,210,000 just enough to cover the previous losses.
And these condors are going to require a collective 31,290,000 in capital more than 28,000,000 more capital than was required by the very first trade just three days earlier.

And so as some kind of a sick Christmas present on Christmas Eve the SPX index rallied yet again closing at 69,30,205 blowing again well past the location of both calls resulting in a loss of over $31 million.

Resulting in a cumulative loss over $50 million in four days and of course as it always inevitably will many of the community members apparently simply ran out of money and some of them were as a result in financial ruined.

And so what are the lessons that we can draw from this very sad story well though is obvious and glaring one is that any system predicated on ballooning capital for each losing trade until you make up all the losses from all the previous cumulative trades that has a giant flaw on it doesn't it.

And that is that there's no one with unlimited amounts of capital it may sound like a foolproof plan you just keep doubling and tripling and quadrupling down on the same bet until the finally wins which it eventually will but the flaws that you won't be there to take that final bet that wins it all back because you'll have gone into bankruptcy.
In other words you won't be able to make that bet and so then the entire thing collapses.
The Martin Gale system may or may not work in gambling I don't know but it sure as hell doesn't work in trading and so this is a cautionary tale.
